deck_meta.title: Economics of AI · What 81,000 Users Said
deck_meta.subtitle: Connecting AI exposure, productivity gains, and job-displacement concerns from the Anthropic Economic Index survey
deck_meta.deck_audience: Enterprise leaders, HR & workforce strategists, AI policy teams

slides.title: Economics of AI
slides.subtitle: What 81,000 Claude users told Anthropic about productivity, exposure, and the fear of displacement

slides.title: From What AI Does → To How Users Feel About It
slides.subtitle: First large-scale qualitative bridge between observed Claude usage and worker sentiment
slides.key_message: Prior Anthropic work measured what Claude is used for. This survey measures what those users feel.

from_block.label: FROM
points: Economic Index: tasks Claude performs
Observed exposure: % of a role's tasks Claude touches
Quantified usage — not perceptions
No causal link to worker sentiment

to_block.label: TO
points: 81,000 self-selected Claude users surveyed
Open-ended interview inside Claude.ai
LLM classifiers extract occupation, sentiment, stage
Direct link between exposure data and felt threat

items.label: Findings & Trends
items.sub: Three headline patterns from the survey

items.label: Core Views
items.sub: Sentiment tracks exposure · career stage matters

items.label: Thematic Analysis
items.sub: Who fears AI · who actually benefits

items.label: Actions & Recommendations
items.sub: Five concrete moves for enterprise leaders

slides.title: Findings & Trends
slides.subtitle: What the data shows
preview: High AND low wage groups gain the most

slides.title: The Headline Numbers
slides.subtitle: Productivity gains are real and large — anxiety scales with exposure

stats.label: Mean productivity rating
stats.context: Self-reported on 1–7 scale · 5 ≈ substantially more productive

stats.label: Voice job-displacement concern
stats.context: 20% of respondents directly expressed worry about losing their role

stats.label: Cite scope · speed gains
stats.context: Scope: “I'm a non-tech guy but now I'm a full-stack developer”

slides.narrative: Two stories sit on top of each other: AI makes people meaningfully more productive — and the same people, especially those most exposed, are increasingly nervous about what comes next.

slides.title: People's Intuitions Match the Usage Data
slides.subtitle: Worker anxiety is concentrated where Claude is most active — not paranoia
slides.primary_quote: People's intuitions track the usage data: they worry most about AI's effect in the jobs where we observe Claude doing the most work.
slides.secondary_quote: Like anyone who has a white collar job these days, I'm 100% concerned, pretty much 24/7 concerned about losing my job eventually to A.I.
slides.implication: Workers can feel the diffusion of AI in their own occupations long before formal labor statistics show displacement. The fear is rational — and concentrated where Claude is most active.

slides.title: Three Patterns Behind the Headlines
slides.subtitle: Productivity, threat perception, and benefit flow each follow distinct distributions

cards.body: The more AI does in your occupation, the more you fear displacement. Linear, measurable, and consistent.
cards.evidence: +1.3pt threat per 10pt exposure. Top quartile mentions worry 3× as often as bottom quartile.

cards.body: Highest- and lowest-wage occupations report the largest productivity gains. Middle-wage gains least.
cards.evidence: Software developers report top gains — but so do customer service reps building side businesses.

cards.body: Early-career workers are simultaneously more anxious and less confident they personally benefit.
cards.evidence: Only 60% of early-career say they personally benefit, vs 80% of senior professionals.

slides.title: Scope and Speed — Not Just Speed
slides.subtitle: Most users describe AI unlocking new abilities, not just faster execution

left.label: SCOPE · 48%
points: “I'm a non-tech guy but now I'm a full-stack developer”
Customer service rep starts e-commerce business
Landscaper builds a music application
Users attempt tasks they could not do before

right.label: SPEED · 40%
points: “15 minutes that used to take 2 hours” — accountant
Faster code reviews, contract checks, paperwork
Quality gains via more thorough verification
Cost gains: replace work that exceeded budget

slides.callout: AI is not just an efficiency tool — for many users, it is an access tool. The distinction shapes who gains and how.

slides.title: Speedup and Anxiety Form a U-Shape
slides.subtitle: Both extremes — being slowed down and being sped up the most — correlate with the highest job threat

left.label: LEFT TAIL
points: Creative workers find AI stifling and rigid
Still fear AI diffusion into their field
Examples: fine artists, certain writers
Highest threat perception among groups

right.label: RIGHT TAIL
points: Largest measured productivity gains
Above-baseline threat perception
Logic: if tasks shrink rapidly, what is left?
Speed gain does not equal job security

slides.callout: The narrative “AI just makes good workers more productive” misses half the story. The most-empowered users are also the most worried.
slides.source_note: Source: Figure 6 · U-shaped relationship between speedup level and perceived job threat

slides.title: Strategic Insights
slides.subtitle: Hidden structural risks behind the positive top-line

slides.title: Two Long-Term Structural Risks
slides.subtitle: Today's positive sentiment hides questions about who actually captures the gains

cards.body: Most workers say benefits flow to themselves — but a meaningful share report employers extracting the gains.
cards.evidence: 10% of respondents who named a beneficiary said their employer or clients were the ones getting more work from them.
cards.implication: Productivity gain ≠ wage gain. Without bargaining power, the surplus accrues to firms, not workers.

cards.body: The cohort with the most anxiety is also the cohort least confident they personally benefit — a structural mismatch.
cards.evidence: Only 60% of early-career workers see themselves benefiting (vs 80% of senior). Hiring of recent grads already slowing in the U.S.
cards.implication: The traditional ‘climb the ladder’ career path may be eroding from the bottom rungs first.

slides.title: Actions & Recommendations
slides.subtitle: What enterprise leaders should do now

slides.title: Five Moves for Enterprise Leaders
slides.subtitle: Workers' fear of AI is rational and predictable — you can act on it

actions.what: Build your own observed-exposure map: % of each role's tasks done by AI today.
actions.value: Identify which teams will feel threatened first · prioritize change management

actions.header: Focus on Scope, Not Just Speed
actions.what: Position AI as an access tool enabling new tasks — rather than a labor-cost cutter.
actions.value: Aligns with the 48% who frame AI as scope-expansion · reduces displacement framing

actions.what: Create AI-augmented apprenticeship programs that explicitly protect early-career development.
actions.value: Counters the early-career squeeze · preserves the future senior pipeline

actions.what: Explicit gain-sharing schemes — bonuses, equity, or time-off — tied to AI-driven productivity wins.
actions.value: Closes the employer-capture gap · converts anxiety into engagement

actions.what: Run sentiment listening explicitly with highest-speedup workers. Don't assume they feel safest.
actions.value: Heads off attrition in highest-leverage staff · turns U-shape insight into retention

slides.title: The Big Picture
slides.subtitle: AI is empowering and unsettling its users at the same time — the two feelings live in the same people

pillars.body: The first large-scale qualitative data linking observed AI usage to user sentiment. Productivity gains are real, broad-based, and skewed bimodally across the wage distribution.
Worker anxiety is not paranoia — it tracks measurable exposure. The most-empowered users are simultaneously the most worried. Career stage shapes who benefits.
Beneficiary capture (who keeps the surplus) and the early-career squeeze (who gets in) are the two structural risks behind the positive top-line numbers.

slides.closing_line: The opportunity is not just to deploy AI — it is to do so in a way that lets the workforce share both the upside and the certainty.

slides.title: References
slides.subtitle: Primary source, methodology, and where each statistic appears

items: Massenkoff, M. & Huang, S. (2026). What 81,000 people told us about the economics of AI. Anthropic Research / Economic Index.
Companion: Anthropic Economic Index March 2026 Report.
Sample: 81,000 self-selected Claude.ai personal-account users (acknowledgements cite 80,508).
Format: Open-ended interview embedded in Anthropic Interviewer inside Claude.ai.
Inference: Claude-powered classifiers extract occupation, career stage, productivity, threat, beneficiary.
5.1 mean productivity rating — §Who benefits from AI?
1 in 5 voice job-loss concern — §Who worries about displacement?
U-shape between speedup and threat — Figure 6
